content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
SUPERSEDED
Practices Commission
‘° (1O2- I (3)(41)
November 8,
1993
Dennis Dart
Law Offices of Brenton A. Bleier
1001
C Street, Suite 101
Sacramento,
CA
95814—0834
RE:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
File No.
1—93—371
Dear Mr.
Dart:
This is in regard to your letter requesting general
assistance on behalf of Larry Wagner with respect to the conflict—
of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
You indicate in your letter dated October 26,
1993, that you have
been authorized to request advice on behalf of Mr.
Wagner.
Since your request does not pertain to a particular governmental
decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
For
example,
in your letter dated September 30,
1993,
you include
facts pertaining to Mr.
Wagner’s appointment as Executive Officer
of the Sacramento Regional Fire/ENS Conuuunications Center.
We
have not considered those facts in providing you informal
assistance.
In addition, this letter is based on the facts
presented to us.
The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
Moreover,
our advice is limited only to provisions of the
Act.
We cannot provide advice about other conflict—of—interest
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California
Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—371
Page
2
laws,
such as Government Code Sections 1090 and 1126.
You may
want •to contact the Attorney General’s Office regarding those
laws.
QUESTION
Does Mr. Wagner’s salary as Executive Officer of the
Sacraiuento Fire/EMS Communications Center
(“SFRECC”) disqualify
him from participating in governmental decisions in his capacity
asa member of the Board of Directors of the American River Fire
Protection District
(“ARFPD”),
of which SFRECC is a.member?
CONCLUSION
Mr. Wagner’s governmental salary from the SFRECC does not
disqualify him from participating in governmental decisions in his
capacity as a member of the Board of Directors of the ARFPD with
respect to decisions affecting the district.
However,
a conflict
of interest may exist where a decision will result in his personal
expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities or those of his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing by $250.
FACTS
Larry Wagner is currently a member of the District Board of
Directors of the ARFPD and Executive Officer of the SFRECC,
of
which ARFPD is a constituent member.
The SFRECC is a joint powers
agency organized under the Joint Exercise Powers Act,Sections
6500 et seq., which is empowered to appoint a person to the
position of Executive Director pursuant to a joint powers
agreement.
Prior to Mr.
Wagner’s appointment as Executive Officer of the
SFRECC,
Mr.
Wagner was a member of the
SFRECC Governing Board,
as
a representative of ARFPD.
On September 22,
1993, the
SFRECC
Governing Board appointed Mr.
Wagner to the Executive Officer
position he now holds.
Mr.
Wagner resigned from his position as
ARFPD representative on the Governing Board of the SFRECC on that
date.
-
Mr.
Wagner is a salaried employee of the SFRECC,
a joint
powers agency,
and under the ARFPD conflict of interest code,
a
designated employee of the district.
ANALYSIS
One of the purposes of the Political Reform Act is to ensure
that public officials perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free from bias caused by the own financial interests or the
financial interest of persons who have supported them.
(Section
81001.)


Our File No.
1-93—371
Page
3
In furtherance of this goal, the Act prohibits a public
official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision
in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103
provides in pertinent part:
An official has a financial interest in. a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(c).
(Emphasis added.)
As a member of the District Board of Directors of
the .RFPD and as Executive Officer of the SFRECC,
a joint powers
agency,
Mr.
Wagner is a public official under the Act.
(Sections
82041 and 82048; Elliott Advice Letter,
No.
1—90—917.)
According to your facts,
Mr.
Wagner has a fixed salary from
the SFRECC.
Section 82030(b) (2),
which defines “income,” provides
that salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received
from a state, local or federal government agency is expressly
exempted from the definition of “income” for purposes of the Act.
Consequently, the salary Mr.
Wagner receives from ARFPD is not
considered income under the Act which will create a conflict of
interest for Mr.
Wagner with respect to decisions affecting
ARFPD.
3
However,
Section 87103 states that an official has a
financial interest in a decision if the decision has a material
You have not provided any facts which indicate whether
the exclusion under Section 82030(b) (2)
is applicable to
Mr.
Wagner as a member of the Board of Directors of the ARFPD.
However,
you have indicated that as a member of the board of
directors,
he is a designated employee of the ARFPD.
Thus, if
Mr.
Wagner receives salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem from ARFPD, the exclusion is also applicable.


Our File No.
1—93—371
Page
4
financial effect on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family.
Thus, the Act also provides that a conflict of
interest may exist where a decision will result in a public
official’s personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities or
those of his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by
$250, irrespective of the source of the increase or the decrease.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (4); Schreiber Advice Letter,
No.
A-88—459;
Torrance Advice Letter,
No.
1-89-142; Fitzpatrick Advice Letter,
No.
A—93—308;
Brown Advice Letter,
No.
A—93—261.)
Please note that our advice is limited only to provisions of
the Act.
We cannot provide advice about other conflict-of-
interest laws which may be applicable,
such as Government Code
Section 1090
(prohibition on contracting with official’s own
agency)
or Government Code Section 1126
(the doctrine of
incompatible offices).
If you have any questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division

QUESTION: Does Mr. Wagner’s salary as Executive Officer of the
Sacraiuento Fire/EMS Communications Center
(“SFRECC”) disqualify
him from participating in governmental decisions in his capacity
asa member of the Board of Directors of the American River Fire
Protection District
(“ARFPD”),
of which SFRECC is a.member?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Wagner’s governmental salary from the SFRECC does not
disqualify him from participating in governmental decisions in his
capacity as a member of the Board of Directors of the ARFPD with
respect to decisions affecting the district.
However,
a conflict
of interest may exist where a decision will result in his personal
expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities or those of his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing by $250.
